sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Gregory, presents with a non-healing foot ulcer on his right foot, which he has had for approximately two months.
The ulcer has recently become red and is draining.
He reports that he may have gotten the ulcer from walking barefoot on the beach at Dewey Beach.
He denies any specific pain, but states that the area sometimes throbs.
His biggest concern is the redness and drainage.
He has not had this type of issue before.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, but does not regularly check his blood sugar levels.
His last A1c level is reportedly around 10%.
He has not seen his primary care physician (PCP) recently, but was started on antibiotics before coming to our office, which he has not yet picked up from the pharmacy.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional throbbing pain in the affected area, but does not have any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Normal vital signs, including blood pressure, pulse rate, oxygen saturation, and temperature, are not explicitly mentioned, but the patient does not have a fever.
Physical exam: The patient has a one by two inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint.
The wound is red, draining, and has some edema around it.
There is fluid present, but no necrosis, odor, or bony exposure.
Test Results: An X-ray of the right foot shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with an infection, likely due to his poorly controlled diabetes.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Suspected infection; Poorly controlled diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed clindamycin 400 milligrams twice daily for seven days to treat the infection.
I will also perform a debridement to remove dead tissue from the wound.
The patient will need to wear a surgical shoe to avoid putting pressure on the affected area.
I will see the patient back in two weeks for another debridement and to assess the progress of the wound.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in two weeks to reassess the wound and consider further treatment.
The patient is instructed to call the office if he experiences any issues or if his condition worsens.
